data.title: Yuanbao: Could Be A Good Time To Get In Due To Trade Uncertainty
data.description:
Yuanbao (NASDAQ:YB), an insurance distributor using the latest technology like artificial intelligence or AI in China, released its most recent or Q1 FY2025 report on June 5, which made the quarterly report the first to be released since YB went public with an IPO in late April. A lot of information about YB was released as needed in order to go public, but the latest report gives investors an up-to-date snapshot of how well YB is doing and to gauge whether YB is worth considering as an investment. Moreover, if the Q1 FY2025 report is indicative of what to expect from YB, then long YB has a lot going for it. Why will be covered next.

YB competes in a competitive, but growing environment

The insurance market in China is large and filled with a number of big players. Chief among them is the market leader, China Life Insurance, which with 150-200K employees is among the 100 largest companies in the world according to Forbes 500, but it also includes other giants like Ping An insurance. Their huge size allows for them to offer a wide range of insurance products, although they also engage in other financial services as well.

In contrast, YB is much smaller than the aforementioned companies, and it does not offer as wide a range of options as a result, mostly health and accident insurance, but it can still compete for customers interested in insurance policies by targeting niches in the market. For instance, unlike when the aforementioned companies were founded, customers no longer need to visit offline locations where the established players like China Life are dominant due to the large numbers of locations at their disposal and the huge number of employees to service them.

Newcomers would need to invest huge sums to replicate this offline network, which is something not many can afford. Instead, YB went for a lower-cost approach by focusing on the Internet segment, where it is easier to compete with established players because their legacy network of offline locations is not as important for reaching out to the public. YB has its own website and portal at its disposal, in addition to social media marketing using the WeChat app. In doing so, YB can focus on potential customers that are likely to convert because they are already interested in obtaining insurance, which is more efficient.

This tech-heavy approach promises a number of advantages. For starters, faster growth is one of them. According to a recent report from Tsinghua University's School of Finance, released earlier this year and which included YB among its participants, the Internet insurance sector in China is expected to grow at a CAGR of 15-20% in the next five years to reach one trillion RMB in premiums, which is much faster than the industry average or Chinese GDP growth. In comparison, the sector grew at a CAGR of 32% in the ten years between 2013 and 2023.

YB can aim for a wide reach with this Internet model, which can be approximate that of an offline network to a significant degree in order to grow faster than otherwise would be possible, on top of other advantages like cost reduction that YB hopes to achieve using this approach. Not only does YB not require as much real estate, for instance, but it also requires fewer workers. The big insurance providers tend to be burdened by their size, but YB, due to its smaller size, is able to be more nimble and react quicker, which allows for YB to quickly incorporate the latest in technology. For instance, YB uses LLM-based AI models to answer customer queries, which reduces the need for workers. All this is to reduce costs and improve profits in general, in combination with faster growth.

YB comes to life with an assist from a strong earnings report

YB went public on April 30 by pricing its stock at $15 per ADS. The stock rose initially, but for much of the past 1.5 months following the IPO, the stock traded below its IPO price. However, YB has moved higher in recent days and the stock is now worth almost a fifth more than the IPO price after YB closed at $17.95 per ADS.

The stock was not worth much more than the IPO price as recently as June 4 when it went for $15.45 per ADS, but it is unlikely to have been a coincidence that YB has since shot higher because it happened shortly after the release of the Q1 FY2025 report on June 5. The market seemed to have liked what it saw in the latest report, judging by the movement in the stock that followed, and it is not hard to see why. The Q1 FY2025 report showed how YB is a company on the rise.

YB is in the midst of a strong expansion

For instance, Q1 FY2025 revenue grew from RMB674.5M in Q1 FY2024 to RMB970.1M or $133.7M in Q1 FY2025, an increase of 43.8% YoY. GAAP net income grew from RMB132.85M to RMB295.1M or $40.7M, an increase of 122.1% YoY. In terms of non-GAAP, which excludes share-based compensation expense, adjusted net income grew by 103.2% YoY from RMB153.6M in Q1 FY2024 to RMB312.2M or $43.0M in Q1 FY2025.

This translates to GAAP earnings of RMB6.46 or $0.89 per ADS, up from RMB2.95 a year ago. Insurance policies grew by 21.3% YoY to 6.8M in Q1 FY2025. YB finished Q1 FY205 with cash and cash equivalents of $308.13M, partially offset by $2.2M of debt. There was also another $71.07M in short-term investments and time deposits on the balance sheet.

In comparison, according to the prospectus released prior to the IPO, YB grew revenue in the last completed fiscal from RMB2,045.15M in FY2023 to RMB3,284.53M or $449.98M in FY2024, an increase of 60.6% YoY. Net income more than quadrupled from RMB205.2M in FY2023 to RMB865.8M or $118.6M in FY2024.

Going back further shows how revenue grew from RMB470.97M in FY2021 to RMB1,121.64M or $162.62M in FY2022 and net earnings flipped from a loss of RMB441.51M in FY2021 to a net profit of RMB18.75M or $2.72M during this time. It’s fair to say YB is growing the top and the bottom line by leaps and bounds. The top line alone, for instance, grew at a CAGR of 91.1% in FY2021-2024.

Yet multiples for YB are not high. For instance, sales totaled RMB3,580.04M or $493.34M TTM, which means YB is valued at 1.6x TTM sales with a market cap of $809.19M. Net income totaled RMB1,028.1M TTM, which converts to RMB22.81 or $3.14 per ADS since the total number of ADS/ADRs outstanding is about 45.08M. This accords YB a P/E ratio of 5.7x with the stock trading at $17.95 per ADS, which compares very well against most stocks.

Could YB be undervalued at current levels?

According to the methodology favored by people like Peter Lynch, YB, with a P/E ratio of 5.7x, would need to grow earnings by more than 5.7% per year, on average, for at least the next three years for a PEG ratio of less than one and to be considered undervalued relative to growth. For instance, if YB managed to grow earnings at a CAGR of just 6%, then that would be enough to give YB a PEG ratio of 0.95x, or less than one.

Such a desired growth rate of better than 5.7% per year does not look like a target that is out of reach for YB. Earnings grew at a much faster pace than 5.7% in recent years as shown earlier. Net income jumped from $2.72M in FY2022 to $118.62M in FY2024, for instance. While this does not guarantee earnings will grow just as fast in the coming years as they did in past years, it does provide a measure of confidence that YB is capable of growing at the desired rate.

For example, even if we were to be very conservative with our forecasts and assume FY2025 earnings stay flat compared to Q1 FY2025, which is almost certainly to be an underestimate, then that implies FY2025 net income of RMB1,180.4M or $162.8M, which represents a 37.3% increase compared to the RMB865.85M or $118.62M earned in FY2024.

If YB then grows earnings at a modest CAGR of 10% in the next three years, which is just a fraction of what it achieved in the previous years, and we assume the estimate of FY2025 net income of $162.8M or $3.61 per ADS is accurate, then that implies a fair value of $36.11 using the same aforementioned methodology. This is well above the current share price of $17.95 per ADS.

Note that if YB manages to grow earnings significantly faster than a CAGR of 10%, which is quite possible, then fair value would come in even higher. Keep in mind that China is still relatively under-covered when it comes to insurance penetration compared to more mature economies like the U.S. or Japan. China is already the second-largest insurance market, but with more growth opportunities, YB should have a good shot at a CAGR of 10%, and likely even better.

The point is that 10% is enough to make an argument YB looks undervalued at current levels. So even if we assume earnings slow down, if only because comps get harder with a higher base, YB is unlikely to grow slow enough for PEG to come in at above one, at which point people like Peter Lynch would agree YB can be argued to be overvalued relative to growth.

Are there risks to be aware of relative to YB?

There is a case to be made that YB is undervalued relative to growth, which argues in favor of long YB. Nevertheless, there may be reasons for potential buyers to remain hesitant about YB. For instance, one potential reason could be the ownership structure of YB. Shares of YB are divided into two classes, A and B.

The latter are owned by the current CEO/chairman of the board, which gives him more than 90% of the total voting power, even though he owns about 40% of all shares. This means that buyers of A shares do not have much of a say, which might be a dealbreaker for potential buyers. In theory, decisions could be pushed through that go against the interest of most shareholders.

Potential buyers of YB will also have to contend with owning ADS/ADRs, which could also be problematic for some investors because of the limited protection they provide to shareholders. YB is also a Chinese stock trading on a U.S. exchange, which means YB could be negatively affected if there is a dispute between the U.S. and China.

This came to the forefront in recent months because of the dispute around tariffs, which likely played a role in the lackluster performance of the stock post-IPO. Things got better when tensions eased and the two countries started talking again, but tensions could escalate again. It’s also worth mentioning that there have been calls to delist Chinese stocks, which includes YB, in the U.S., which could also make potential buyers think twice about putting money in a stock that could get stuck in legal limbo, even if it is just temporarily.

YB makes heavy use of modern technology, including the latest in generative AI, which can reduce costs and boost earnings growth by extension, but this exposes YB to regulatory changes in China. If the Chinese government imposes caps on how AI can be used, then that could create hiccups for YB, which could affect how fast earnings grow because there may be additional costs imposed on YB that need to be taken care of.

YB is also a fairly young company that has not even been listed for two months. Some may want to wait to see how YB performs over a longer period of time before stepping in on what they may regard as a bit too much of an unknown quantity, even with the prospectus in hand. At this time, there is only one quarterly report available with which to assess YB, which some may feel is not enough to draw conclusions.

Investor takeaways

YB has not done all that great in the few months following the IPO in April, but the stock seems to be on the move, likely with the help of an easing in trade tensions and a quarterly report that showed how there is a lot to like about YB. YB is a fast-growing company with relatively high net profit margins at over 30%, which are desirable attributes in a stock. In the Q1 FY2025 report, revenue grew by over 40% and profits more than doubled YoY.

YB’s revenue has grown at a rate roughly three times as fast as the Internet insurance segment in China, which by itself is also growing faster than the insurance industry as a whole. Its net profit margins of over 30% are also much higher than the leading players like China Life or Ping An, which are in the high single digits to low teens or below. This suggests that YB’s approach, especially as it relates to keeping costs down as much as possible, primarily by utilizing the latest in technology, while seeking faster growth, is working.

This by itself is likely to be appealing, but YB can also be acquired at relatively low multiples, including a P/E ratio in the mid single digits. This means that YB needs to grow earnings at a CAGR of better than 5.7% per year in order to be considered undervalued relative to growth, which is a target that YB is very likely to achieve, and even exceed by a wide margin, especially if the Tsinghua report is on point with its growth forecast for Internet insurance.

Even if earnings grow by just 10% per year, fair value for YB could be twice what the stock goes for. Double the growth rate and fair value could be even higher. CAGR is very unlikely to come in at just 5.7% to get a PEG of one and to be considered fully valued, let alone overvalued if PEG rises above one.

I am thus bullish on YB. There are a few blemishes on YB that are not ideal, including a lot of voting power in the hands of one individual. The state of U.S.-China relations is also a wild card for YB. It seems to have weighed on the stock in recent weeks, and this could happen again in the future, depending on how the relationship evolves.

On the other hand, the stock’s tepid performance following the IPO, despite YB arguably having what it takes to send the stock on a rally with its combination of low valuations and fast growth, could be argued to be an opportunity to take advantage of. If U.S.-China relations had been better, the stock likely would have traded higher than it did. This could be seen as an opportunity to get in on a stock that ought to be trading higher than where it can be found right now. If this is accurate and the stock is trading lower than it would have otherwise, then now could be a good time to take advantage and get in on YB.

Bottom line, it is possible YB may be held back to a certain extent by trade disputes, and bulls may have to wait for the stock price to catch up, but the combination of relatively low valuations and fast growth is too good to pass up. It may take time to be rewarded for long YB, but with the state of the income statement and the balance sheet, one can be confident there may be a reward.

data.title: Park Hotels & Resorts Inc Could Be A Value Trap, Hold
data.description:
Setting The Stage

As the hospitality sector rebounds, Park Hotels & Resorts Inc (NYSE:PK) is under-whelming, currently trading at $10.9 near its 52-week low of $8.27. The stock is trading at a significant discount, and I believe its recent asset sales strategy to invest in high return assets is aimed at unlocking value for investors. The company owns a premium portfolio of 39 hotels and resorts with about 25,000 rooms across prime US markets like Hawaii and New York, among others. While the company’s capital recycling approach is a common practice in the REITs industry, I think its success hinges on the timing, market dynamic and execution. Looking at its underwhelming performance recently, I think the current undervaluation could be a value trap. As such, I recommend holding this stock as we assess the execution of its capital recycling approach and until notable impacts are felt on its financials.

Financial Snapshot: Underwhelming

On May 5, 2025, this company reported its Q1 2025 earnings and I find it unattractive. While some could argue that $630 million in revenue beat estimates by about $14 million and this could be a positive sign, I will refer you to the consistent negative growth in revenue and the weakness in other metrics. To begin with, while I acknowledge the revenue beat in Q1 this year, it’s important to note that the YoY growth came in at negative 1.41%. What concerns me most is that for more than 5 consecutive quarters, the company has reported negative revenue growth and even worse, over the last five years, the overall trajectory is downwards. This is an indication of a weakening business model.

The problem is not the top line only! Its EPS for the quarter was -$0.29 missing estimates by $0.33. This alongside a net loss of $57 million compared to $29 in net profit in Q1 2024 is an indication of deteriorating profitability. Indeed, this raises questions about earnings visibility and the company’s ability to generate sustainable returns.

More troubling, operating income plunged to about $7 million down from $92 million which is approximately 93% decline from last year. This is a clear indication of that operations are under pressure. With a 1.1% operating margin, down from 14.5%, it is clear that the buffer against further shocks is thin.

Most importantly, its adjusted FFO per share, which is a key metric for REITs fell by more than 11% to $0.46. This double-digit slump suggests reduced distributable income, which puts pressure on its dividend sustainability.

The most likely question here is what are the reasons behind the weakening performance and is it likely to persist? Based on my assessment, the status quo is arising from structural and operational headwinds. For instance, looking at the ADR which rose by about 2.3%, occupancy dropped by approximately 2.1% resulting in 0.7% RevPAR decline. In my view, the mismatch here suggests a potentially weakening pricing power and costs inflation is potentially outpacing revenue growth.

Looking into the future, I think the near term outlook is bleak. I will give a few reasons for that. To begin with, the $1.4 billion in debt wall come 2026 is a looming refinancing challenge. Looking at its weak liquidity with cash at $233 million and trailing cash from operation at $423 million, I think the most likely avenue is refinancing. However, interest rates are still high and EBITDA is already under pressure. As a result, refinancing at favorable terms is almost elusive.

Secondly, Hawaii's weakness is likely to persist. It is important to note that RevPAR in Hawaii dipped by about 15% YoY. The drop was majorly due to occupancy decline of 11.8 points. Although some of it was strike related, international travel recovery remains uneven and group booking are ramping up slowly. As a result, the weakness in this market could persist through 2025.

Above all, its downward revision in guidance signals an internal caution, and this to me implies that we should not expect a quick rebound. This is also backed by 5 downward revisions in consensus FFO against zero upward revisions and 9 downward revisions in consensus revenue against one upward revision.

Asset Sales And Reinvestment: The REIT’s Strategy Missing The Mark

PK is planning to sell between $300-$400 million in non-core assets in 2025 and redirect the proceeds to high ROI projects such as property upgrades. From where I sit, I see several indications that this ambitious project may fail to have meaningful impacts. In the first place, the company is reinvesting in high-cost environment. The Federal Reserve has maintained its rates between 4.25%-4.5% since December. I am looking at this initiative against the current total debt of $4.79 billion and with a weighted average maturity of 2.9 years. From a different perspective, the high-interest rates result in high borrowing costs, which allude to the affordability crisis of the reinvestment projects resulting in low occupancy.

I think that unless the rates come down substantially, the high borrowing cost could erode ROI, especially if the renovations fail to deliver higher RevPAR.

Another notable aspect that most analysts seem to miss is the shifting consumer preference. With the emergence of Airbnb, conventional focused hotels are facing a threat to occupancy rates and pricing power. The demand for these facilities is gaining popularity and their demand is surging.

Although the company’s focus on premium properties majorly in major urban cities could mitigate this, it remains a broader challenge in the industry.

Counterargument

Although the company is faced with weak financials and its current capital recycling strategy is faced with several some risks, the strong leisure travel recovery could serve as a growth catalyst for the company. Since 2020, the expenditure has been rising consistently from $346.3 billion to $927.2 billion in 2024.

Given this consistent growth, which is estimated to grow by a CAGR of 3.91% by 2029, I think if the company implements its new strategy well it could seize the opportunities in the recovering industry.

Valuation & Market Sentiments

Considering the asset intensive nature of the REIT industry, I will value this stock using the NAV. Assuming its total assets of $8901 million, total liabilities of $5465 and the 199.89 million in outstanding shares, the company’s net asset value per share is $17.19, this is a significant discount compared to its current price of $10.19.

However, in my opinion, the current undervaluation is a value trap considering its poor financial performance. Although its planned asset sale is aimed at changing the status quo, it is faced with significant risks and its execution and success are not guaranteed. Above all, the company has significantly underperformed the S&P 500 over the last three years, warranting its current valuation.

In addition, the company’s short interest currently stands at 13.39% and has been rising consistently in the recent past, an indication of growing market skepticism.

Conclusion

As I wrap up, I recommend holding PK because although the company is pursuing new avenues to turn the status quo around, there exists significant challenges, specifically its high debt and the elevated interest rates. Although the industry recovery is strong, the company fundamentals are yet to reflect the positive impact of this recovery. Until that happens, I remain unmoved.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: Index Down For Second Straight Week
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 posted its second straight weekly loss, finishing down 0.2% from the previous Friday. The index is now sitting 2.87% below its record close from February 19th, 2025.

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached a then all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53.

This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new then all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

And here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above the 50-day moving average since May 1st and above the 200-day moving average since May 12th. Additionally, the 200-day moving average has been above the 50-day moving average since April 14th.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. On April 9th, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (10.77%) since December 24th, 2018 (19.10%). Also included is the 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. Over the past 20 days, the average percent change from the intraday low to the intraday high is 0.93%.

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index which includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently up 1.69% year to date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 1.41% year to date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Winners And Losers Of The Bifurcated Housing Market
data.description:
By Rich Hill, Global Head of Real Estate Research & Strategy

The ramifications of normalization in the U.S. housing market to pre-pandemic levels, at least in some regions, are substantial to investors. Though elevated interest rates, dampened affordability, and policy uncertainty remain concerns, there are pockets of investment opportunities, particularly in the commercial real estate (CRE) space, where income returns have outweighed capital returns. This dynamic is likely to continue even amid slowing home price appreciation in the period ahead.

As the run-up in post-pandemic home prices slows, bifurcations in U.S. real estate present both risks and opportunities for investors.

Homes in the post-pandemic boom areas of the West Coast, Mountain West, Florida, and even Texas are finally seeing inventory levels surpass 2019 levels. A shortage of inventory previously fueled prices in some regions. However, as for-sale inventory flips to oversupply, it’s creating a difficult environment for sellers as demand remains lackluster given affordability challenges.

A counterintuitive dynamic that could exacerbate matters for U.S. housing, at least in some regions, is a decline in interest rates. Sellers could become more incentivized if improving affordability spurs greater demand. This could weigh on housing valuations as they normalize lower to a more balanced supply vs. demand environment.

A lower-rate environment, however, would favor commercial real estate (CRE) valuations. In fact, year-over-year changes in CRE total returns turned positive in 4Q24 and continued to accelerate higher in 1Q25 to +2.8%, given a combination of still solid fundamentals, improving lending conditions, and interest rates that have declined from peaks in late 2023.

Going forward, income is likely to remain the main driver of CRE total returns. This dynamic resembles the real estate market in the decades leading up to the 2008 crisis versus the somewhat financially engineered returns following that period post-GFC, which were driven by historically low interest rates.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Intel Posts Widest Q1 EPS Beat Among S&P 500 Companies
data.description:
Intel Corp.'s (INTC) normalized earnings per share in the first quarter exceeded the consensus projection by a wider margin than any other S&P 500 company.

The technology products and services company, known for its semiconductor manufacturing, reported normalized EPS of 13 cents in the first quarter - more than 2,700% above the consensus estimate of just over zero cent, according to an analysis of S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

Intel attributed the stronger-than-expected EPS to its higher revenue, stronger gross margin and lower operating expenses in the quarter, Executive Vice President and CFO David Zinsner said during an earnings call April 24. The company also shuffled its leadership team in March.

Conversely, Crown Castle Inc. (CCI) reported a normalized loss per share of $1.06 in the first quarter, falling short of the consensus projection for a loss of 1.2 cents per share - the largest percentage EPS miss among S&P 500 companies.

Crown Castle owns, operates and leases cell towers and fiber cable networks. It is in the process of selling its fiber business and undergoing a leadership change.

Midsize, small companies post largest EPS beats

After Intel, the next four S&P 500 companies that exceeded normalized EPS estimates to the largest degrees were Ford Motor Co. (F), Carnival Corp. & PLC (CCL), Dow Inc. (DOW) and Live Nation Entertainment Inc. (LYV). All five companies are defined as midsize or small companies within the index.

EPS estimate beats from Intel and Ford topped all midsize companies in the S&P 500 that had market capitalizations between $30 billion and $100 billion at the end of the first quarter.

Ford's earnings exceeded expectations due to lower costs and strong net pricing in North America, according to CFO Sherry House. During an earnings call in May, the company disclosed that it was suspending guidance for the full year 2025 due to risks and uncertainty associated with US tariffs.

Carnival reported the largest earnings beat among S&P 500 companies with market capitalizations under $30 billion. The company posted normalized EPS of 13 cents in the first quarter, exceeding the consensus estimate of 2 cents.

Performance of large companies

The most significant EPS outperformances among the S&P 500's largest companies were reported by The Boeing Co. (BA), Uber Technologies Inc. (UBER), Booking Holdings Inc. (BKNG), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Pfizer Inc. (PFE)

Boeing's normalized loss per share of 49 cents was 62.4% higher than the consensus estimate for a loss of $1.30 per share, representing the best EPS surprise among companies with market capitalizations over $100 billion at the end of the first quarter.

The aerospace and defense manufacturer mitigated its loss in the quarter due to strong commercial product deliveries and improved operational performance, Executive Vice President of Finance and CFO Brian West said during the company's earnings call held April 23.

S&P 500 Q1 earnings growth exceeds estimates

The aggregate EPS of all S&P 500 companies in the first quarter climbed 13.1% year over year. This exceeded an estimated 6.4% increase based on data compiled April 9 shortly after the end of the first quarter.

However, the index's first quarter EPS gain was less than the 16.2% year-over-year jump in the fourth quarter of 2024.

"First-quarter corporate earnings exceeded expectations, and capital expenditures remain robust," Carl Ludwigson, managing director of Bel Air Investment Advisors, said in an email to Market Intelligence. "However, the competing forces of uncertain tariff impacts and pending tax and regulatory relief continue to cloud the outlook, creating a wide range of potential scenarios."

Year-over-year EPS improved in seven of the S&P 500's 11 sectors, led by healthcare with a 46.5% gain.

Aggregate EPS in the energy sector dropped the most, with an 18.0% year-over-year decline. However, the S&P 500 Energy index posted a return of over 9% during the first quarter, leading all of the S&P 500 sector indexes.

The consumer discretionary sector achieved a collective EPS improvement of 6.6% in the first quarter, despite facing economic headwinds and ongoing investor risk aversion.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ASA: The Bullish Trends For Gold Continue, Profit From Them
data.description:
ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited (NYSE:ASA) is a Bermuda-based closed-end fund that investors can purchase as a means of getting exposure to gold prices without the need to purchase the precious metal itself. This is something that could prove to be a more attractive proposition than many readers might think, given that gold has actually outperformed domestic common stocks since the start of the 21st century. We can see that here:

The spot price of gold is up 1,181.13% since January 1, 2001. Meanwhile, an investor who purchased the S&P 500 (SP500TR) on the same date and then reinvested all dividends, distributions, and other payments into additional units of the S&P 500 Index would only be sitting on a 630.57% total return. In other words, gold has outperformed the S&P 500 Total Return Index by a fairly significant margin despite the fact that it has no yield and the financial media and financial authorities (such as central bankers) try to discourage investors from purchasing it. As a recent article on Zero Hedge stated, the mainstream financial press and governmental monetary authorities frequently dismiss precious metals while attempting to encourage investments in highly valued stocks and government bonds that have negative real returns. The chart and long-term price history largely speak for themselves, though, and clearly, we can see that investors would have been better off buying gold than buying common stocks during most periods of the 21st century.

Despite the fairly strong run-up that gold has already had since the turn of the century, there are still very strong cases to be made for its continued outperformance, and we will discuss those throughout this article. For now, though, we can see here that ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited has delivered a performance over the past ten years that has correlated pretty well with the price of gold:

We can, of course, see that the fund’s performance compared to gold has not been a perfect correlation. In particular, the fund was outperforming gold by quite a lot over the 2020 to 2022 period before falling back. In addition, it has slightly outperformed gold since the start of 2024. Overall, though, we can see that it has proven itself to be a fairly good way to profit from a rising price of gold while still earning some income along the way.

Unfortunately, though, the distribution yield of ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited has historically been very low when compared to other closed-end funds. As of the time of writing, the fund only yields 0.15%. This is a lot lower than the yield of the S&P 500 Index (SPY) or most other domestic indices, as we can see here:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

S&P 500 Index

1.20%

Dow Jones Industrial Average (DIA)

1.52%

Russell 2000 Index (IWM)

1.18%

As regular readers will immediately notice too, the 0.15% yield of ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited is substantially lower than that of most of the closed-end funds that we have previously discussed in this column. It is certainly lower than that of most of its peers:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited

Equity-Sector Equity

0.15%

Adams Natural Resources Fund (PEO)

Equity-Sector Equity

9.52%

BlackRock Resources & Commodities Strategy Trust (BCX)

Equity-Sector Equity

8.84%

GAMCO Global Gold, Natural Resources & Income Trust (GGN)

Equity-Sector Equity

8.07%

The lower yield does not necessarily mean that ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited generates significantly lower returns than its peers. In fact, ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited was the best-performing fund in its peer group over the past ten years:

For the purposes of constructing this chart, I used total returns as opposed to price returns, so we can see that the much higher yields of the peer funds did not result in them providing a significantly higher return to their investors. In fact, investors in all of the peers would have been less wealthy at the end of the ten-year period than was the case for investors in ASA Precious Metals Limited.

The biggest reason for ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited’s low yield relative to its peers is that it is a Bermuda-domiciled fund and, as such, is not subject to the regulations set forth by the Investment Company Act of 1940. In particular, mutual funds (and other types of regulated investment companies) are required to distribute their ordinary income and capital gains to qualify for special tax rules that are available to them. ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited does not have this requirement, and the fund does not pay out all of its realized capital gains and net investment income. This distinction allows the fund to retain its investment profits in order to redeploy them into other investments. This might actually give it an advantage over its peers if it is able to generate higher returns on its capital than its shareholders can. Historically, this has been the case, as the fund’s performance clearly shows. After all, this is one of the few closed-end funds that has managed to beat the S&P 500 Total Return Index over the past ten years:

Anything that can outperform the broader large-capitalization domestic equity market over a ten-year period deserves our attention. This is particularly true if the fund is able to continue to deliver a high level of performance going forward. Fortunately, there could be some reasons to believe that gold itself, as well as the shares of ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited by extension, can continue to achieve high levels of performance going forward. The remainder of this article will provide the rationale for that conclusion.

Recent Performance Update

We previously discussed ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited on April 4, 2025. As such, about two and a half months have passed since our previous discussion on this fund. There have been a few developments in the market since that time, some of which have been quite positive for gold and other precious metals. We can see that by looking at the price of various precious metals since that date:

This chart shows the spot prices of gold (black line), silver (blue line), and platinum (orange line) since our last discussion on April 4, 2025. As we can see, all three metals are up fairly significantly over the two-and-a-half-month period. Gold is the laggard, however, and there could be a good reason for that. Over the past few years, gold has been one of the better-performing assets available in the market. The spot price of gold has risen 93.64% over the past three years, substantially beating the S&P 500 Index. Silver and platinum did not do as well, however, as silver only rose 87.90% and platinum only rose 53.04% over that period. The S&P 500 Index is up 55.46% over the past three years, to put this in perspective.

Historically, the prices of silver and platinum have both correlated with gold. This historical relationship is known as the “silver-to-gold ratio” and the “gold-to-platinum ratio,” respectively. Due to this correlation, the three metals will usually deliver similar performance over the long term, although the performance of the three can differ in the short term. Usually, what happens is that gold will rise first because it is the first one that comes to most people’s minds whenever they are thinking about precious metals, and, of the three, it is the most likely to be purchased by central banks and other huge institutional buyers. That will result in gold outperforming both silver and platinum for a time. It also means that after such a period of outperformance, silver and platinum will actually outperform gold since they need to “catch up” in order to remain within their historical range to gold, so to speak. Basically, what happens is that speculators will begin buying silver or platinum whenever they perceive a given metal as being cheap relative to gold, and this drives up the price of the other two metals. That is probably what we have been seeing over the past few months.

My previous article on ASA Gold and Precious Metals was published on April 4, 2025, which was two days after the United States shocked the market by imposing tariffs on most of its trade partners. The imposition of these tariffs caused investors to panic and pull their money out of common equities and put it into a perceived safe haven. However, as I have pointed out in a few other articles, the safe haven asset of choice was not U.S. Treasuries. This was a departure from what we have seen in previous market crashes. Rather, in this case, investors mostly purchased gold as the safe haven asset of choice. This is one of the reasons why gold prices have risen by more than 10% over the past two months. Another notable event that occurred over the intervening period was the outbreak of hostilities between Iran and Israel, which also prompted a flight to safety. Once again, gold and other precious metals were the assets of choice, as investors are growing increasingly hesitant to purchase U.S. Treasury securities in the wake of the Moody’s downgrade and the Congressional Budget Office’s projections that cast doubt on whether or not the United States will ever get its fiscal house in order.

It was not only retail and private investors who have been purchasing gold as a safe haven asset, either. CNBC reported last week that gold is now the second-largest holding in the reserves of central banks around the world (second only to the U.S. dollar). This is a departure from the previous state of affairs that saw the U.S. dollar and euro as the top holdings. It is worth noting though, that most of the increase in gold actually came at the expense of the U.S. dollar and not the euro:

As we can see here, the euro and currencies other than the euro and U.S. dollar have actually been fairly stable as a proportion of global central bank reserves over the past five years or so. It is the U.S. dollar that has seen its proportionate representation on global central bank reserves decline. This is unlikely to be a surprise to regular readers, as I have pointed out the declining dominance of the U.S. dollar for several years now, but this does clearly show how far the trend has progressed.

This trend could also continue to push the price of gold ever higher, due at least in part to the fact that central banks are not particularly price sensitive. These organizations do not need to consistently generate positive returns, nor can they ever run out of money. The presence of such large, price-insensitive buyers can very easily push the price of gold when measured in fiat currencies ever higher, due to the simple fact that gold cannot be simply printed out of thin air as fiat currencies can. There is no reason to believe that central banks will cease their gold buying activities anytime soon. In fact, they seem to be continuing to purchase and repatriate their gold. Germany just recently demanded that the Federal Reserve return 1,200 tons of gold that it has been keeping in storage in New York to Germany. Due to gold leasing programs, it is uncertain whether or not the Federal Reserve actually has the German gold in its vault and if it does not, then it will have to purchase it. The demand for physical, instead of merely paper, gold is therefore another driver of gold prices. Surveys show that approximately 68% of central banks are planning to reduce their U.S. dollar exposure and buy gold that will be stored in their own vaults. Currently, estimates suggest that a single ounce of gold may have ten to twenty institutions, all with claims against it. This is the reason why some institutions have started demanding physical delivery, and that alone is bullish for prices because banks and other counterparties have to actually purchase the physical gold in order to honor their commitments. In the past, it was easier to simply settle with cash and not worry about where the gold being sold would be sourced. As we have already seen, ASA Gold and Precious Metals tends to benefit from a rising gold price, so we can see how these trends would all be positive for this fund overall.

As gold, silver, and platinum prices have all risen since our last discussion, it would be logical to assume that the share price of ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited has also risen. This is indeed the case, as shares of this fund are up 31.63% compared to a more moderate 17.87% gain for the S&P 500 Index:

Thus, ASA Gold and Precious Metals continued its recent trend of beating the S&P 500 Index over the past few months. Most investors will likely appreciate this, as everyone likes to outperform the benchmark indices. Of course, there is no guarantee that the fund will be able to continue this attractive performance indefinitely going forward, but as we have already seen, gold has done pretty well against common stocks over the past twenty-four years, and this fund’s share price has a high correlation to the price of gold. There are certainly reasons to be fairly bullish on gold and other precious metals right now, so this could all work in the fund’s favor going forward.

About The Fund

The fund’s website states that:

ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited is a closed-end precious metals fund.

The fund focuses primarily on investing in gold mining companies, but it has a particular focus on small- and medium-capitalization companies. Also from the website:

It is our opinion that with the increasing price of gold, companies with better leverage to gold exist in the small and mid-capitalization and that the portfolio will exhibit improved leverage to the price of gold by investing in such companies; as a result, we have increased select holdings in the small and mid-cap space. These companies, while showing improving margins and solid balance sheets, also have growing production and development pipelines relative to the larger cap names.

The management of ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited is hardly alone in this belief. Many articles published here on Seeking Alpha and on other sites state that junior miners can be a more profitable way to play rising prices of a given resource than senior miners. This has actually proven to be the case with gold miners over the past three years, as can be shown here:

This chart shows the performance of the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX), which tracks senior gold mining companies, against the performance of the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ), which tracks junior gold mining companies, over the past three years. This is a period of time in which gold prices were rising, so it should work pretty well for our comparison. As we can see, the junior gold miners outperformed their larger brethren over the period. This generally reinforces the statement that small-capitalization and mid-capitalization mining companies could deliver better performance than larger companies as the price of gold keeps rising. Thus, the fund could be making the correct decision here, assuming that it is actually doing what it claims to be doing.

Unlike many closed-end funds, ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited provides a list of all of its holdings in its most recent fact sheet. Here is the list:

Admittedly, this list is dated April 30, 2025, so it is not quite as recent as it could be, but there is no reason to believe that the fund has changed its holdings much over the past month. The fund’s most recent annual report states that it only had a 24% annual turnover in the twelve-month period that ended on November 30, 2024. This is actually a bit higher than the fund has had in previous years:

FY 2024

FY 2023

FY 2022

FY 2021

FY 2020

Portfolio Turnover

24%

10%

13%

17%

31%

(all figures are for the twelve-month period that ended on November 30 of the respective year)

These are all generally very low turnover figures for any actively managed closed-end funds, so we can assume that ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited does not change its portfolio very often. This is fine, as it strongly suggests that the fund is mostly running with a buy-and-hold strategy as opposed to a trading strategy. This helps to keep transaction expenses down and can deliver superior returns over time, so it is perfectly fine to see.

Anyone who is familiar with the mining sector can attest to the fact that many of the fund’s holdings are not senior gold miners with several producing mines and a high level of output. In fact, only 7.6% of the fund’s assets are invested in companies that produce more than one million ounces of precious metals annually:

We can also see that more than half of the fund’s assets are invested in precious metals producers that produce less than 500,000 ounces per year. Thus, this fund is clearly focusing its efforts on smaller mining companies, just like its website claims. As we have already seen, small mining companies can deliver greater returns than large mining companies when the price of gold (or silver or platinum) goes up, so this fund appears to be pretty well positioned for continued strength in precious metals prices.

Distribution Analysis

As is the case with many closed-end funds, ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited provides its investors with a regular distribution. However, this fund does not pay out all of its income and investment profits to the shareholders. Indeed, the fund’s most recent distribution was only $0.03 per share, and the company has only paid out $0.05 per share over the past year. That gives it a 0.15% yield on a trailing twelve-month basis and a 0.18% per share yield on a forward basis, assuming that it continues to pay $0.03 per share semiannually going forward. Obviously, this is not a particularly good closed-end fund for anyone who is seeking to achieve a high level of income from their assets. Rather, this is a fund that is held for long-term capital appreciation.

It is worth noting, however, that ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited has paid much larger distributions in the past:

For example, we can see that back in the 1980s and up to the subprime mortgage crisis of 2008, this fund had substantially larger distributions. Thus, it does not appear that the fund’s organizers are particularly averse to paying outsized distributions; it is simply that the fund is not paying them right now.

Let us have a look at the fund’s financial reports to see how well it is actually doing. As of the time of writing, the most recent financial report that is available for ASA Gold and Precious Metals is the first-quarter 2025 report that corresponds to the three-month period that ended on February 28, 2025. This may be somewhat surprising to some investors, as this is one of the few funds that releases quarterly financial statements as opposed to semi-annual statements.

For the three-month period that ended on February 28, 2025, ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited reported a net investment income of $788,889. The fund did not actually pay a distribution during this particular three-month period, but its annual distribution only costs $1,134,667 at the current share count of 18,911,123 (assuming a $0.06 per share annual distribution). Thus, in only a single quarter, the fund came pretty close to covering its entire annual distribution solely with net investment income. If we include the $6,531,571 net realized gains that the fund had during the three-month period, it easily covered its entire annual distribution in just three months.

ASA Gold and Precious Metals is repurchasing shares, and this is substantially more expensive than its distribution. During the three-month period that ended on February 28, 2025, the fund bought back $2,199,063 of its own shares. The fund was able to cover this solely with the net investment income and the net realized gains.

Overall, there is no reason at all to worry about the fund covering its payments to the shareholders.

Valuation

ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited states on its website that it has a net asset value of $36.53 per share. It also lists a share price of $33.00 per share, which was the closing price on June 18, 2025. This price would give the fund a 9.66% discount on net asset value, although the fund did fall somewhat today. On June 20, 2025, ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited closed at $32.59 per share, which is a 10.79% discount on the most recently reported net asset value, but it is not certain what the fund’s net asset value closed at on June 20, 2025.

ASA Gold and Precious Metals has historically traded with a fairly large discount to net asset value, which is probably due to the fund’s very low yield. This low yield almost certainly reduces the fund’s appeal among traditional closed-end fund investors. Over the past three years, the fund has traded at prices ranging from a 4.77% discount to an 18.45% discount on net asset value. The average price over the period was a 13.24% discount to net asset value, which is a better price than what the fund has today.

Expenses

The fund’s annual report states that it had a 1.67% expense ratio for the full-year period that ended on November 30, 2024. This is far higher than the fund’s historical expense ratio of around 1%:

Year

2024

2023

2022

2021

2020

Expense Ratio

1.67%

1.05%

1.00%

0.94%

1.02%

(all figures are for the twelve-month period that ended on November 30 of the respective year)

The increase for the most recent year is very disappointing, but it is worth noting that the increase came primarily from one-time expenses that the fund incurred. If we exclude these one-time expenses, the fund’s expense ratio drops down to 1.01%, which is a closer approximation to what investors can likely expect from this fund going forward.

Conclusion

In conclusion, gold has been a stellar performer in recent months and recent years, and it seems likely that it will continue to be so going forward. The primary drivers of the bullish trend for gold are still very much in place, and this makes for a very good situation for the companies that are held by this fund. ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited has been one of the best-performing gold mining funds over time, although it is not an especially good option for investors who are seeking a high level of current income. The fund tends to hold on to its capital gains rather than pay them out, and as such, it delivers the bulk of its investment returns through capital appreciation and not distributions. This is not necessarily a bad thing, as investors can still profit from capital gains, it just requires the sale of the stock in order to get spendable money. For anyone who is looking for a way to profit from gold without buying the metal directly, though, ASA Gold and Precious Metals could be an option.

data.title: Slide Insurance: Investors Feel Sure Despite Florida Concentration
data.description:
Shares of Slide Insurance Holdings (NASDAQ:SLDE) have seen a solid public offering as investors are attracted to the combination of technology in insurance, solid growth and strong margins. This looks great, but as the business has been in operations for just about three years, I am somewhat cautious as well given the concentrated risks.

All this make me rather cautious, and while earnings multiples look reasonable, I am cautious given the concentration risks, some specific risks tied to the company, and the fact that I am quite cautious on insurance in general.

A Tech & Specialty Insurance Business

Slide Insurance has only been founded as recent as 2021 as a tech-enabled, fast-growing and coastal specialty insurance firm. The company focuses on single-family homes and condominiums in the property & casualty in the coastal states near the Atlantic. The company specifically focuses on market opportunities which are underserved by traditional insurers, as it is looking to gain new business through various channels and methods.

In particular, this situation applies to Florida, a state in which large national carriers have seen their market share more than cut in half to 30% in the time frame between just 2019 and 2022. The idea is that its technology allows for both the adoption of profitable policies, and avoiding large risks and losses.

This is also the area in which the company focuses, with Florida responsible for 99.5% of policies outstanding here, complemented by tiny exposure on South Carolina. Key in this is that traditional carriers typically have bad insight in reinsurance costs, which the company aims to address.

Investors find comfort in the CEO and under Bruce Lucas, which has a strong track record as CEO and Chairman of Heritage Insurance Holdings (HRTG), with strong growth reported in the 2010s.

Valuation and IPO Thoughts

Slide aimed to sell 24 million shares in a preliminary price range which was set between $15 and $17 per share, with final pricing set at the higher end of the range. With 16.7 million shares offered by the business itself, gross proceeds from the offering which benefitted the company came in at $400 million.

With a share count of 125 million shares, the company is valued at $2.1 billion at the offer price. The balance sheet is a bit hard to read, and will, of course, benefit from the cash proceeds of the offering, but this is due to the nature of the activities.

For the year 2023 the company reported gross premiums written of $874 million, net revenues of $468 million and net earnings $87 million. The company has seen spectacular growth in 2024 with gross written premiums up 53% to $1.33 billion, net revenues up 80% to $846 million, as net earnings more than doubled to $201 million. Based on a share count of 125 million shares, these earnings came in at $1.60 per share, which frankly made valuations look quite appealing.

For the first quarter of 2025, gross premiums written rose by just 14% to $278 million. Net earnings rose in a spectacular fashion to $93 million, but this was largely driven by a positive $72 million contribution from a change in unearned premium, this metric typically being negative. Adjusted for the swing in this item, earnings would be down slightly on an annual basis.

With shares trading at $22 in the days following the offering, the market value has risen to $2.7 billion. Based on earnings power of $1.60 per share, valuations looks quite reasonable based on the earnings multiple (in the high-teens) also given the solid growth of the business.

What Now?

The reality is that the business only wrote its first policy as recent as spring 2022, and ever since has seen written premium exceed a billion in less than 3 years' time. This growth is a key risk, but there are more, including a focusing on technology, highly concentrated activities in around Florida, which results in potential adverse effects relating to weather. Moreover, the Florida housing market is suffering from special assessment charges following building collapses, but there is more.

Other than generic competitive and regulatory risks, there is also the risk of some parts of the business model. This includes weather, excessive payments to its CEO, as well as the fact that many policies were acquired from the State fund, called State Florida Citizens Insurance.

At current valuations around $2.7 billion equity valuation (as the enterprise valuation is hard to estimate here) the business is valued at around 2 times gross written premiums and 3 times net revenues, accompanied by solid earnings. In comparison, Heritage trades well below revenues in terms of net revenues, this being the predecessor of its CEO.

Amidst all this, I see some fundamental support from an earnings point of view, but as I am not big on insurance, I am cautious, certainly given the idiosyncratic risks given the focus on Florida here.

data.title: Newsmax Won't Outfox Fox News And Is Overvalued
data.description:
Some months ago, Newsmax (NYSE:NMAX) was worth $30 billion, even topping Fox News’ valuation. While share prices have descended dramatically from such lofty heights, I believe NMAX remains overvalued, with a market cap now hovering around $1.6 billion. At the right price, this could be an interesting company to invest in. Yet even in best-case scenarios, there are strong headwinds that will need to be overcome for Newsmax and any traditional (or, at least, semi-traditional) media/publishing company.

Further, Newsmax, in particular, faces a tough uphill battle against Fox News (FOX, FOXA). While the company has secured a solid core audience, future growth may prove difficult in the face of stiff competition. Wisely, Newsmax recognizes that traditional media outlets are not necessarily the future, even if they are currently cash cows. Yet in this space, too, I believe the company will face stiff headwinds.

It’s Tough Sledding for Traditional Media

First, some context. Newsmax relies on traditional broadcasting for most of its revenues at the moment. This is especially worrisome, in my view, because traditional broadcasting is likely to decline as those older cohorts who still rely on traditional media shrink. The hardest hit so far have been newspapers. Countless newspapers have been closed down. Many smaller papers have been bought out by larger syndicates, but while they still provide local coverage, it’s often not as extensive.

Television has fared better, but the clock may be ticking for traditional TV as well. Older generations watch more traditional TV than younger generations, as illustrated below. As older cohorts shrink, Newsmax may find its primary revenue under pressure. It’s possible that as people age, they’ll turn to their TVs more often. GenX may start to consume more TV and spend less time online, but it seems more likely to me that they’ll continue to use the media forms they’re currently relying on.

Cable TV is going to be a tough market for Newsmax. To Newsmax’s credit, the leadership team recognizes that they can’t put all of their eggs in the cable TV basket and the company is making a strong push into streaming services. Still, the revenue breakdown above suggests that digital revenues are not growing quickly.

On cable TV, Newsmax is a relatively small fish compared to other cable news networks, as I will explore below. In the online space, there are tons and tons of small shows, bloggers, etc. and NMAX is a relatively big fish compared to most of them. The vast majority of smaller bloggers and podcasters will ultimately end up failing, at least from the perspective of making substantial sums of money. Still, for NMAX, it doesn’t matter if 99.99 percent of small operations fail to evolve into serious competitors. If .01 percent rise from the fray to secure large audiences while having far lower operational costs, it'll make the operating environment more difficult.

Newsmax can bring in some rising stars in the space, but it’s not going to be cheap. Many of these rising stars will know that they are bringing a lot of financial value to the table, and while a larger organization like Newsmax can lend credibility, in the increasingly “democracized” media space, that credibility likely isn’t as valuable as it once was.

All of that said, Newsmax2, a free streaming channel, did report 25% viewership growth (YOY) in May. While online media will offer opportunities, I believe it will be difficult for NMAX to monetize this content effectively, especially relative to production costs. Small channels and bloggers are likely to have substantially lower production costs.

Can Newsmax Outfox Fox?

Another serious issue for Newsmax is that much of its growth will likely have to come by seizing market share from Fox News. Both serve the same general demographic, more or less. Newsmax is generally considered to be more conservative than Fox and less restrained in their commentary. Given the political climate, which has demonstrated increasing polarization, I believe this has been reflected in Newsmax’s quick growth.

When Fox News let go of Tucker Carlson and made other moves to rein in commentary following Joe Biden’s win in the 2020 election, many Fox viewers felt betrayed and fled the network. Newsmax’s strong growth seems to have been due in large part to catering to this audience, which was a sound business strategy. Yet since then, Fox News has started to recover viewership and is no longer bleeding viewers. In fact, Fox News has been seeing strong numbers.

Fox News reports that in May weekday primetime viewership hit 2.9 million, the highest out of all news networks. The network also reported 1.6 million total day viewership numbers, 20% growth from May 2024, while MSNBC dropped 33% and CNN lost 22% in the same time frame. Fox News also outperformed in the crucial 25-54 demographic, pulling in 180,000 total day viewers, whereas CNN secured just 59,000 and MSNBC pulled in only 49,000. Newsmax data hasn’t been reported on as far as I can find, but the key takeaway here is that Fox News is going to be a tough company to overcome.

USTVDB does provide stats for Fox, CNN, MSNBC, and Newsmax. While Newsmax emphasizes that it is the #4 cable news network, as we see below, the gap with not only Fox, but also CNN and MSNBC, is large.

Fox News Ratings Last 3 Months

CNN Ratings Last 3 Months

MSNBC Ratings Last Three Months

Newsmax Ratings Last 3 Months

That said, when we step back to look at Newsmax’s performance over the last few years, the growth is evident. However, growth may be leveling off. This is especially likely given that FOX is starting to trend higher.

Newsmax’s Ratings Performance Since March 2022

Fox’s Ratings Performance Since March 2022

Newsmax also mentions social media followers as evidence of value. Social media is important, but these numbers are also relatively easy to manipulate. Regardless, I did a quick comparison between Newsmax and Fox News.

  • X/Twitter- FOX= 26.5 million NMAX = 3.4 million
  • Facebook- FOX = 26.4 million NMAX = 5.2 million
  • Truth Social- FOX = 3.3 million NMAX = 4.57 million

Newsmax does win on Truth Social, which is a conservative-leaning social media site, so that’s good news. Still, the massive gap between FOX and NMAX on X/Twitter and Facebook is hard to ignore.

Fundamentals and Best Case DCF Calculations

Newsmax’s fundamentals on Seeking Alpha, by and large, don’t look great right now. Growth metrics are strong, so if Newsmax can sustain growth, its numbers could improve considerably. That said, I don’t believe that NMAX will be able to sustain such growth.

Currently, revenues YOY are growing at more than 20%, far above the 2.34% sector median. Levered FCF Year Over Year growth is an astounding 166%. However, keep in mind that these numbers are starting from a relatively low base. Revenues are growing quickly, yes, but NMAX’s TTM revenues still total only $175 million (vs. $16.1 billion for FOX).

The balance sheet is healthy, which I believe is especially important for a smaller company looking to grow, like NMAX. Cash & ST Investments total $216 million, while total assets top $282 million. Total liabilities are just $100.8 million. NMAX’s net income margin is negative 22%, so the company could end up burning through cash rather quickly.

As for DCF calculations, I did some quick calculations using Alpha Spread’s DCF calculator. I wanted to create as favorable of a valuation as I could justify to try to grasp NMAX’s highest potential value. Right now, Newsmax’s net income margin is -22%, whereas Fox News’ is 11.54%. Within the calculator, I adjusted NMAX’s net income margin to 12%, to reflect similar profitability to Fox News.

This is an extremely generous assumption, but again, I wanted to try to consider the highest (somewhat) logical valuation. (I also set the discount rate at 10%, which I use for most stocks to make comparisons a bit easier. Terminal growth was set at 4%, to reflect typical global GDP growth rates.) Even with such generous assumptions, NMAX falls far short of its current stock price as illustrated below.

NMAX Equity Model via FCFE

NMAX Equity Model via Net Income

I also did a quick DCF calculation for Fox News to get a better grasp now Newsmax stacks up against its chief competitor. The news for Newsmax wasn’t good, and using Fox News’ current data and a 12% net income margin (same assumptions), I find that FOX may be a bit undervalued right now, as the DCF calculator suggests that shares are worth $62.5. Currently, Fox’s shares are selling for ~$50. I wouldn’t rate FOX a buy on that alone, but I do plan to circle back at some point to examine FOX as an investment.

FOX Equity Model via FCFE

Key Take-Away: Newsmax Has a Tough Road Ahead

I believe that NMAX is overvalued based on its current metrics and data. The company is enjoying strong growth, yet even if it can maintain growth through the near future, it will be difficult to justify its current price. Any slowdown in growth is likely to result in steep selloffs as well. So, despite NMAX’s steady price decline, I don’t believe it offers good value and thus is a sell.

data.title: The CoWoS Chokepoint: TSMC's Quiet Monopoly On AI Memory Bandwidth
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome LL Insights as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

TSMC (NYSE:TSM) is widely recognized as the foundry linchpin of the AI boom, yet one aspect of that dominance is just beginning to reshape the competitive landscape: control of three-dimensional advanced packaging capacity and its deliberate replication outside Taiwan. CoWoS lines in Taichung, hybrid-bonded SoIC stacks in Hsinchu and soon-to-arrive system-on-wafer assembly in Arizona and Japan have created a supply-chain choke point no rival can match. Those assets, rather than transistor leadership alone, now drive pricing power, margin integrity and geopolitical optionality. A fresh look at the numbers indicates that the market is still undervaluing this emerging moat by roughly 80%.

Generative models are doubling appetites for logic density and memory bandwidth every twelve months, but transistor counts only create value when logic can talk to memory at terabyte-per-second speeds. TSMC alone can mass-produce 3 nm silicon and stitch it to twelve-high HBM4 stacks through CoWoS-L and hybrid bonding at commercial yield. Capacity already covers roughly 70% of Nvidia’s 2025 demand and is on track to reach 70,000–75,000 wafer-equivalents per month by year-end, almost 3× 2023 output. By synchronizing node shrinks with packaging ramps and duplicating that stack across new sites in Kumamoto and Phoenix, management is converting a technology lead into a global utility position that competitors cannot replicate within the investment horizon.

Advanced Packaging Flywheel

The CoWoS and SoIC families are now the bottleneck that determines whether a next-generation GPU ships on time. TrendForce data show CoWoS volume rising to at least 70 000 wafers a month in 2025, with internal targets as high as 75 000. Each Blackwell B200 package consumes six to eight N3E logic dies plus up to twelve HBM4 base dies, turning a single processor order into more than a dozen high-margin steps that only TSMC can run at yield.

Hybrid-bonded SoIC amplifies that leverage. Presentations at the 2025 Technology Symposium outlined a move to face-to-face copper-to-copper interconnects with 6 µm vias by 2029, a twenty-fold I/O density jump versus today’s mainstream µ-bump flows. AnandTech notes that SoIC designs in the pipe now exceed 30 tape-outs for 2026-2027, pushing TSMC’s hybrid capacity to double next year and double again by 2027. Because defect density falls when dies shrink, every incremental SoIC wafer yields disproportionately higher good die, reinforcing a virtuous cycle of lower cost and higher customer lock-in.

Competitors are years behind. Intel Foundry’s glass-core roadmap remains in R&D, while Samsung’s X-Cube throughput and pitch lag CoWoS by a full node equivalent. Reuters reported that TSMC is even weighing a CoWoS module in Japan to extend the moat geographically. Until those challengers solve yield at scale, TSMC’s packaging flywheel keeps spinning faster and wider.

Geographic Bridgehead Strategy

Roughly 70% of wafer capacity still sits within 100 miles of the Taiwan Strait, but the picture is changing. Fab 21 in Phoenix started pilot 4 nm runs in early 2025 and is slated to add a 3 nm phase before the decade’s end, with room for 2 nm modules under construction. Each phase is paired with an advanced assembly plant that will copy CoWoS-L process steps, giving North-American hyperscalers a secure domestic source for AI packaging.

In Japan, the JASM joint venture with Sony, Denso and Toyota began 12-nm volume in December 2024 and is ramping a second 6 nm line to lift capacity to 100 000 wafers a month by 2027. Reuters reports that Tokyo is courting TSMC to bolt CoWoS modules onto that site, leveraging Japan’s substrate and equipment ecosystem while harvesting subsidies that lighten the capex load.

The result is a portfolio that mitigates Taiwan risk without exporting crown-jewel 2 nm know-how. Arizona handles 4–3 nm, Japan 12–6 nm and advanced packages, while gate-all-around and below remain anchored at Hsinchu and Tainan. That asymmetric replication preserves trade secrets, earns government incentives and reassures OEMs whose boards increasingly demand multi-region supply security.

Financials

First-quarter 2025 net revenue reached NT$839 billion, up 42% year-on-year, while gross margin expanded to 59% and operating margin to 49%. Operating cash flow hit NT$626 billion and capex swallowed NT$331 billion, leaving NT$295 billion of free cash in just one quarter. Cash and short-term investments climbed to NT$2.39 trillion against total liabilities of NT$2.53 trillion, keeping net leverage close to zero.

Management guides US-dollar revenue growth in the mid-20% range for 2025 despite Arizona start-up expenses, an outlook reinforced by Barron’s coverage of sustained 3 nm demand and potential price increases for CoWoS services. Every one-point mix shift toward packaging adds 50–70 basis points of gross margin, cushioning overseas dilution.

Valuation

Market cap fluctuates around US $910–930 billion, while enterprise value sits near US $855 billion. Gurufocus lists trailing P/E at 27.5 and EV/EBITDA at 15.3, both modest premiums to five-year averages. Those multiples look lean next to Nvidia at 39× EBITDA and AMD near 34×. Intel trades at 82× EBITDA because earnings have collapsed, while Samsung sits near 10–11× forward earnings but lacks leading-edge share and remains memory-cycle dependent.

A discounted-cash-flow model makes the undervaluation starker. Starting with the NT$295 billion Q1 free cash flow, annualized to NT$1.18 trillion and converted at the Treasury’s March 31 rate of 33.2 NT$ per dollar, baseline FCF is US $35.5 billion. Assuming 25% growth in 2025, 20% in 2026, fading to 4% by 2034 and a 3% terminal rate, discounted at 9%, the present value of operating cash totals about US $1.10 trillion. Subtracting net cash yields an equity value of roughly US $1.02 trillion, or US $380 per ADR. The key sensitivity is packaging growth: If CoWoS volume rises only 15% a year, fair value still lands near US $320, implying 50% upside.

This intrinsic valuation complements relative metrics. Price-to-sales at 10.3× trails Nvidia’s 35×, while PEG sits near 0.5 even on consensus estimates, signaling that the market discounts TSMC’s growth durability.

Challenges Remain

Geopolitical turbulence remains the headline concern. A kinetic Taiwan Strait scenario would disrupt a significant portion of production, but the newly established bridgeheads in Japan and the U.S. lower worst-case downtime to specific nodes rather than total output, a nuance often missed in market chatter.

Capital intensity is another risk. Overseas fabs initially shave 2%–3% off corporate gross margin, and further CoWoS duplication could pressure near-term returns. Yet customer prepayments and government subsidies as seen in both Phoenix and Kumamoto transfer a significant slice of that burden off-balance-sheet.

Lastly, valuation multiples could compress if investors rotate out of AI beneficiaries. However, EV/EBITDA of 15× is below the sector’s high-growth peer group average near 25×, while free-cash-flow yield remains above 3%, giving the stock fundamental cushion against sentiment swings.

Final Thoughts

Advanced packaging is no longer a bolt-on service; it is the scarce infrastructure that unlocks trillion-parameter AI. By owning the majority of global CoWoS and SoIC capacity, and by methodically cloning that capability in subsidy-rich geographies, TSMC has created a second moat around its transistor lead. Financial results already reflect that shift in rising cash flow and expanding gross margin.

Relative valuation frames the opportunity: 27× earnings, 15× EV/EBITDA and 10× sales for a company compounding FCF above 20% contrasts sharply with Intel’s triple-digit EBITDA multiple and Samsung’s technology lag. Absolute valuation tells the same story. A conservative DCF anchored on reported free cash flow and packaging-driven growth yields a US $380 intrinsic price, implying roughly 80% appreciation potential.

When silicon performance depends on stacking, interconnect density and proximity to hyperscaler campuses, TSMC is the only foundry offering the full stack at scale. Until a rival can yield 2 nm gate-all-around and 70,000 CoWoS wafers a month in multiple countries, the path of least resistance for both earnings and share price points up. The stock therefore merits a continued overweight for investors seeking durable exposure to the infrastructure layer of the AI economy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SMA Solar: OPEX Cuts And Working Capital Release Should Help A Stock Rebound
data.description:
Investment overview

Before going into the Q1 FY25 earnings, let’s give some context on the business case. SMA Solar (OTCPK:SMTGY)is a German company that produces solar inverters based on string technology, which is particularly well-suited for large-scale infrastructure in the Utility space. In that domain, SMA enjoys a strong position in the US. On residential and commercial solar installations, the firm has been particularly affected by elevated interest rates, which have impacted customer access to leasing, overstocking resulting from the 2022-2023 supply-chain crisis, and weak economic trends in Europe.

Like its Western peers, SolarEdge (SEDG) and Enphase (ENPH), SMA is also selling batteries to its customers, as well as EV chargers and related services.

America's exposure, primarily related to the US, is at 80% due to large-scale projects. Two events contributed to SMA reaching a significant market share in the region within the Utility segment: the ban on Huawei and ABB’s divestment of its inverter branch in the same year, 2019. The two major competitors remaining are Power Electronic, a Spanish firm, and Sungrow, a Chinese firm with local production plants. SMA's presence in the US residential and commercial segments is marginal. SolarEdge and Enphase are dominating the field.

Going to Asia, back in Q4 FY23, management said, “In our APAC region, we continued to face challenging Asian competition.” The segment decreased from EUR 140 m to EUR 130 m year-over-year. We can guess it was referring to Chinese firms such as Huawei, Sungrow, Qinlong, Growatt, and GoodWe. As can be seen below, they have been gaining significant market share, particularly in large-scale unity projects.
