We could, therefore, expect the Asian exposure to remain challenged by these Chinese competitors.

Recent quarterly trends

During the Q1FY25 conference call, management provided some interesting comments on the cost-cutting program in place: “Of the planned up to EUR200m, we aim to realize approximately 40% already this year.” Remember that the program was launched back in September 2024 and will involve the layoff of 1100 employees.

The residential and commercial segments continued their decline due to excess channel inventories in Europe and weak economic conditions. Every year, these two business units experienced a drop of nearly two-thirds in revenue. Such collapse has been observed in a similar magnitude for its closest peers, SolarEdge and Enphase, and is indeed macro-driven. The management gave a small sign of hope, stating: « We do see a slow uptake in order.. slowly but surely ticking up .»

Fortunately, large-scale Utility projects are mainly driven by a robust US market. Management comments on the US Utility market were positively biased: « With our biggest market Large Scale, which is 40% to 50% of our revenue.. we don’t see an underlying problem and are rather bullish that I will then continue once we get clarity into the tariff situation ».

On the backlog side, SMA recorded EUR 1.3 billion in orders. Stripping EUR320m of service, the net backlog covers two-thirds of FY25 expected revenues.

On the positive side, the Q1 FY25 result highlighted a solid cash flow from operational results, reaching EUR 118 m, and was also aided by a working capital release (+ EUR 95 m impact). While more receivables were collected and fewer payables were due, inventories still consumed some cash, as the solid development of the Utility segment requires significant investments. On the contrary, I expect residential and commercial operations to release inventories in the coming quarters gradually.

The level of working capital recorded in the balance is particularly elevated, as inventories remain above EUR 600 m, nearly twice the level of 2022 when the European energy crisis began.

Net of EUR 14 m in CAPEX, Q1 Free Cash Flow stood at EUR 96 m, helping to improve the company's financial position. SMA Solar finished the quarter with EUR 300 m in cash on the balance sheet and EUR 145 m in financial liabilities, including leases.

What valuation can we expect?

The firm recently announced an extensive cost-cutting program, facing challenging business conditions in both the residential and commercial segments. Cuts and cost optimizations are being implemented and will be rolled out across the entire organization, as shown below. The CEO anticipates up to EUR 200 m in cost savings from 2025 to 2027.

The main hypotheses used are the following:

  • I used the low end of the management revenue guidance for FY25. In the future, I expect the company to grow its sales at a moderate rate, as it is unlikely that its residential and commercial businesses will return to the inflated levels of 2023.
  • I modeled EUR 180 m in EBIT savings from FY24 to FY27, in line with the mid-range of the management forecast.
  • With up to EUR 200 m in excess inventories, I expect a gradual release of working capital in FY25 and FY26.
  • CAPEX should not be a significant concern in the coming years, as SMA already has some excess capacity.

Using a WACC of 10% and a long-term growth rate of +3%, I get a fair value of EUR33/share, indicating more than 60% upside potential. Of course, the level of risk is elevated in this case, and any recovery will depend on the speed of working capital release (strengthening the balance sheet) and the effectiveness of the cost-cutting program's execution.

Risks

SMA Solar is facing several sectoral risks, including elevated interest rates that affect solar panel installations, a high level of competition in the residential and commercial sectors from companies like SolarEdge and Enphase, and Chinese companies employing aggressive pricing strategies. Furthermore, regulatory risk has increased. While it is not materially affected in the US by expected IRA cuts due to its limited exposure to the residential sector, we see that renewable subsidies around the world are under threat.

Conclusion

An elevated level of risk could be offset by a significant margin of safety on the valuation side. Positive catalysts could come from working capital releases and OPEX cuts, which would help improve financials and stock sentiment. I believe the risk-reward ratio is attractive, and I rate the stock as a BUY.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
